title: Welcome
text:
Welcome to my plant blog! This is my first post. Today I saw a Trillium!



Trillium



Trillium
The plant in this image is a Trillium ovatum, commonly called Western white trillium, also known as Pacific trillium, or wake robin. It is in the bunchflower (Melanthiaceae) family and is native to western North America, including the Willamette Valley.
You can tell this is a Western white trillium because of these features:

Single whorl of three broad, heart‑to-diamond‑shaped leaves atop a lone stem
Single white flower with three petals and three green sepals held above the leaves
Bright yellow cluster of stamens in the center of the flower
Growing on a shaded forest floor with conifer litter, moss, and ferns typical of low- to mid‑elevation Portland‑area woods



Similar plants
The Western white trillium could sometimes be confused with Trillium albidum which also has three broad leaves arranged in a whorl and a white three-petaled flower. It also has a similar low, woodland habit. However, Trillium albidum is usually taller and more slender, often has petals that are longer and narrower, tends to have flowers that nod slightly or sit closer to the leaves instead of opening flat and star-like, and is more strongly associated with bottomland or floodplain habitats than the mixed upland forests where Western white trillium is common.



Brief History
The genus name Trillium comes from the Latin tri- meaning “three,” referring to the plant’s pattern of three leaves, three petals, and three sepals. Trillium ovatum is native to the moist forests of western North America, from British Columbia down through California and east into parts of the Rocky Mountains. It has long been noticed by Indigenous peoples and early settlers as one of the first showy spring wildflowers to appear on the forest floor, often marking the real arrival of spring in the Pacific Northwest. Historically, Western white trillium has symbolized purity, the renewal of spring, and, in some traditions, delicate or modest beauty because the flowers bruise and fade to pink or purple when handled or as they age.



Fun Facts

The petals of Western white trillium often turn pink or soft purple as they age, so a patch can show white and pink flowers at the same time.
The plant is very slow growing; it can take 5–8 years or more from seed to first flower, which is one reason picking the flower can seriously set the plant back.
In some folk traditions, trilliums were considered woodland “good luck” charms: disturbing or picking them was thought to anger forest spirits or bring bad luck, a belief that likely helped protect the plants by discouraging overharvesting.




Usage / Toxicity
Trillium ovatum is not considered highly toxic, but like many forest herbs it can cause stomach upset if eaten and is not recommended as food. Some Indigenous groups and early herbalists used various trillium species (including Western white trillium) in small amounts as poultices or teas for wounds, respiratory issues, or as a women’s medicine, which led to old names like “bethroot” for eastern relatives. Modern medical use is not recommended due to limited safety data and the plant’s sensitivity and conservation concerns. Today it is valued almost entirely as an ornamental and as a key component of healthy, intact forest understories.



Invasiveness / Conservation Status
Trillium ovatum is not invasive; it is a native forest wildflower that is locally sensitive to disturbance. In many areas near cities like Portland, populations have declined where people pick the flowers or dig plants for gardens, because removing the single flower and its bracts can prevent seed production and weaken the plant. To conserve it, avoid picking or trampling plants, keep dogs and feet on established trails, and never dig trilliums from the wild—use nursery-propagated stock if you want them in a garden. Protecting mature mixed conifer–hardwood forests with rich, undisturbed soil is the best long‑term way to support this species.

title: Purple blooms
text:
All along certain sections of the path in our greenspace are these beautiful purple blooms. I found out that they are Money Plants!



Money Plant



Honesty
The plant in this image is a Lunaria annua, commonly called Honesty, also known as Silver dollar plant, or Money plant. It is in the mustard family (Brassicaceae) and is native to southeastern Europe and western Asia.
You can tell this is a Honesty because of these features:

Four-petaled purple flowers typical of the mustard family, borne in loose clusters

Broad, soft, heart‑ to oval‑shaped leaves with toothed edges on an upright, somewhat coarse stem

Distinctive flat, round, green seed pods already forming beneath the flowers, shaped like small coins

Colony‑forming habit in shaded, disturbed woodland edges, often among brambles and other understory plants



Similar plants
The Honesty could sometimes be confused with Dame’s rocket which also has purple four‑petaled flowers in spring. It also has roughly similar height and grows in similar woodland or roadside habitats. However, Dame’s rocket is more slender, usually has narrow, lance‑shaped leaves, and its seed pods are long and thin rather than flat and coin‑shaped.



Brief History
The genus name Lunaria comes from the Latin word luna, meaning “moon,” referring to the silvery, moon‑like discs of the mature seed pods. Lunaria annua is native to the Balkans and surrounding parts of southeastern Europe and western Asia, but it has been grown in gardens across Europe since at least the 16th century and later naturalized in North America, including the Willamette Valley. Its common English name “Honesty” likely arose because the translucent pods “honestly” reveal the seeds inside, while “money plant” and “silver dollar plant” refer to the coin‑like look of the dried pods. Historically, the plant has been valued mainly as an ornamental for cottage gardens and dried arrangements, rather than as a food or medicine. In the language of flowers, Honesty symbolizes sincerity, openness, and plain dealing, reflecting the see‑through pods.



Fun Facts

When the seed pods dry, the outer green layers peel away to reveal a shiny, silvery inner membrane; these “silver moons” are widely used in dried flower arrangements.

In European folklore, bunches of Honesty pods were sometimes hung in homes or stables as charms for prosperity and good fortune, their coin‑like discs thought to attract wealth.

The seeds and pods contain oils that have been experimentally investigated as a potential source for industrial lubricants and even biodiesel, though this use has never become widespread.




Usage / Toxicity
Lunaria annua is not considered strongly toxic to humans or pets, though, like many mustard family plants, the seeds contain glucosinolates and should not be eaten in large quantities. Historically it has had minor use as a fodder or oilseed plant, but today it is grown almost entirely as an ornamental. Gardeners value it for its bright spring flowers and especially for its decorative seed pods, which are harvested and dried for arrangements. It is not an important medicinal herb, and internal use is generally not recommended due to limited safety information.



Invasiveness / Conservation Status
Lunaria annua is widely naturalized and locally weedy, but not usually listed among the most aggressive invasive species in the Pacific Northwest. In shady urban woods and riparian corridors around Portland and the Clackamas River, it can form dense patches that crowd out some native spring wildflowers, especially where soil has been disturbed. To limit its spread, deadhead or remove plants before the seed pods fully mature and scatter, bag and dispose of seed‑bearing stems, and pull unwanted plants by hand, making sure to remove the roots. Where it becomes thick, replacing it with native woodland flowers such as fringecup, inside‑out‑flower, or native violets can help restore habitat.



Bonus Pic!



Sylvie

title: Fringe Blur
text:
This plant is abundant in our greenspace and is especially noticeable in the spring when it produces rows of little bell-shaped blooms on the ends of tall stalks. The flower stalk wiggles a lot so it’s hard to get a picture in focus. Here’s the best one I got. I’ll try to get a better picture later.



Fringe Cups



Fringe Cups
The plant in this image is a Tolmiea menziesii, commonly called piggyback plant, also known as youth‑on‑age, or thousand mothers. It is in the saxifrage family and is native to the Pacific Northwest of North America.
You can tell this is a piggyback plant because of these features:

Rounded, softly hairy leaves with shallow lobes and scalloped edges on long petioles
Basal rosette of leaves arising from a low crown in a mossy, shaded forest floor
Tall, slender flowering stalk with many small, nodding, greenish to yellow‑brown flowers along the upper portion
Typical Willamette Valley mixed conifer–hardwood understory habitat where this species is very common



Similar plants
The piggyback plant could sometimes be confused with fringecup which also has rounded, scalloped leaves in a basal clump. It also has tall, slender stalks with many small flowers. However, fringecup is usually a bit taller, usually has more showy, bell‑shaped flowers that turn pinkish or reddish as they age, and its leaves tend to have a sharper, more triangular shape with more pronounced lobes and a slightly different, often reddish‑tinged stalk.



Brief History
The genus name Tolmiea comes from William Fraser Tolmie, a 19th‑century Scottish physician and naturalist who worked in the Pacific Northwest with the Hudson’s Bay Company. Tolmiea menziesii is native to the moist forests of western North America from northern California through Oregon and Washington into British Columbia and Alaska, where it has long been a characteristic plant of cool, shaded ravines and streambanks. Its unusual habit of producing new plantlets from the base of older leaves gave rise to its English common names like “piggyback plant” and “youth‑on‑age,” which have made it popular as a curiosity houseplant as well. Historically, piggyback plant symbolizes renewal and generational continuity because the “child” plant literally rides on the “parent” leaf.



Fun Facts

Piggyback plant can clone itself efficiently: small plantlets form where the leaf blade meets the stalk, then root when they touch soil, allowing one plant to gradually form a patch.

It became a minor houseplant craze in the mid‑20th century; people grew it indoors specifically to show off the “baby plants riding on the leaves.”

In some folk traditions in the Pacific Northwest, its “child on parent” leaves were used in children’s charms or garden magic to represent family protection and the safe passage of children into adulthood.




Usage / Toxicity
Tolmiea menziesii is not known to be seriously toxic to humans and is generally considered non‑poisonous, though it is not used as a food plant and should not be eaten in quantity due to lack of study. Its main “use” is ornamental: it is commonly grown as a shade‑loving groundcover in gardens in cool, moist climates and as an indoor foliage curiosity plant elsewhere. There is little strong evidence for medicinal uses in modern herbalism, though Indigenous uses may have included minor topical applications; these are not well documented in the literature.



Invasiveness / Conservation Status
Tolmiea menziesii is not considered invasive in its native range and is generally common to abundant in suitable moist forest habitats. In gardens, it can spread vegetatively into dense patches, so light thinning may be needed to keep it from overtaking smaller plants, but it does not typically become an aggressive weed. Outside its native range it is occasionally naturalized but not widely reported as a problematic invader. To help conserve it and the communities it belongs to, maintaining shaded, moist forest habitat with intact mossy soils and limiting heavy trampling in ravines and streamside areas is beneficial.

title: Brambles
text:
These vines are popping up everywhere in the spring! I always wonder if they are the Himalayan Blackberry, which are highly invasive and go crazy and take over everything, or if they are a gentler native plant. I asked my LLM tool if it could identify the plant in this picture and thought it was the invasive Himalayan Blackberry. However, after more careful examination, and consulting with iNaturalist, I determined that it is actually the California blackberry, which is a native plant that is not invasive. The California blackberry is a much more manageable plant and doesn’t take over everything like the Himalayan blackberry does. I’m glad to know that we have some native blackberries in our greenspace!











(a) California Blackberry











(b) California Blackberry Flower







Figure 1: California Blackberry




California Blackberry
The plant in this image is a Rubus ursinus, commonly called California blackberry, also known as Pacific blackberry, or trailing blackberry. It is in the rose family and is native to western North America, including the Willamette Valley of Oregon.
You can tell this is a California blackberry because of these features:

Slender, trailing canes that root where they touch the ground rather than forming stiff, upright arches
Leaves usually divided into three (sometimes five) finely toothed leaflets with a slightly wrinkled surface
Small, thin prickles along the stems instead of heavy thorns
White to pale pink flowers in spring that develop into small, shiny, dark purple‑black berries with a rich flavor



Similar plants
The California blackberry could sometimes be confused with Himalayan blackberry which also has thorny canes and compound leaves with toothed edges. It also has dark purple‑black berries. However, Himalayan blackberry is much more robust and forms tall, arching hedges, usually has five thicker leaflets with a pale underside, and bears larger, blockier berries; California blackberry stays lower and more sprawling, has finer stems with smaller prickles, and its berries are smaller but often sweeter.



Brief History
The genus name Rubus comes from the Latin word for “bramble” or “bramble bush,” referring to its thorny, sprawling habit. Rubus ursinus is native to the western coast of North America, from southern Alaska down through British Columbia, Washington, Oregon, and California into Baja California, commonly inhabiting forest edges, open woods, and streambanks. Indigenous peoples of the Pacific coast long used the berries and young shoots for food and medicine, and early European settlers quickly adopted it as a favorite wild fruit. The specific name ursinus means “bear-like,” likely alluding to the fondness bears show for the ripe berries. Historically, California blackberry symbolizes abundance and the gifts of uncultivated land, as brambles often mark rich, undisturbed edges of forest and field.



Fun Facts

California blackberry is one of the parents of the famous cultivated logánberry and contributed to the ancestry of several modern blackberry hybrids.
Some Indigenous traditions in the Pacific Northwest used blackberry vines as protective charms; bramble thickets were seen as natural barriers against unwanted spirits or bad luck, and twigs were sometimes placed near doorways for protection.
The flowers are highly attractive to native bees, and dense patches of California blackberry provide important nesting and cover for small birds and mammals.




Usage / Toxicity
California blackberry is not considered toxic and is widely eaten. The berries are used fresh, dried, or cooked into jams, pies, syrups, and wines, and are prized for their strong, aromatic flavor. Young shoots can be peeled and eaten as a spring vegetable. The leaves have been used traditionally as a mild astringent tea for diarrhea, sore throats, and mouth inflammations due to their tannin content. As with many wild edibles, proper identification is essential and moderate consumption is wise for people new to the plant.



Invasiveness / Conservation Status
California blackberry is native and generally not invasive in its home range; it is a natural part of forest edges and riparian vegetation. In gardens it can spread by tip‑rooting and underground stems, so it may need periodic thinning or trellising to keep it in bounds. Where Himalayan blackberry and other invasive brambles have taken over, preserving California blackberry means carefully removing the invasive species while leaving low, fine, native canes in place, and encouraging native shrubs and trees that coexist with this trailing blackberry.




Bonus
In addition to the non-native Himalayan blackberry, the California Blackberry may also be confused with poison oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum). Looking at the leaves can give you clues to telling them apart. The California blackberry has 3 small leaflets, green on both sides, and a round stem with many small straight prickles. Himalayan blackberry has 3-5 large leaflets with white undersides and a 5-angled stem with large, sharp, curved, and widely-spaced prickles. Poison oak also has leaflets of three, but the leaves are not hairy. You can use the fun mnemonic – “Leaves of three, let it be; If it’s shiny, watch your hiney; If it’s hairy, it’s a berry” [source]



Comparison

title: Brambles Part 2
text:
In the last post I shared a pic of the native California blackberry and talked about how they can be easily confused the pesky Himalayan Blackberry, and even my LLM tool couldn’t tell the difference! So this time I thought I would share a picture of the rival Himalayan blackberry, so we can compare them and learn how to spot the differences better. For me the best tip for differentiating them is the number of leaves. The California blackberry has 3 leaflets but the Himalayan blackberry has 3-5 leaflets. So if you look at the whole plant and you see any leaf clusters that have 5 leaflets then you know for sure that you are looking at the Himalayan blackberry. Although they are troublesome, we sure do love to pick their berries in the summer!



Himalayan Blackberry



Himalayan Blackberry
The plant in this image is a Rubus armeniacus, commonly called Himalayan blackberry, also known as Armenian blackberry, or Lawson’s blackberry. It is in the rose family (Rosaceae) and is native to western Eurasia (likely the Caucasus region).
You can tell this is a Himalayan blackberry because of these features:

Large, somewhat triangular leaves with 3–5 deeply wrinkled, serrated leaflets on long petioles
New growth that is light yellow‑green and soft, with the older leaves darker and slightly glossy
Thin, arching cane with small recurved thorns (not very visible here, but typical of this growth form)
Growing as a scrambling cane in a disturbed, partly shaded urban woodland — a classic habitat for this invasive bramble around Portland



Similar plants
The Himalayan blackberry could sometimes be confused with trailing blackberry which also has compound, serrated leaves. It also has prickly canes. However, trailing blackberry is much lower and more vine‑like, usually has smaller and thinner leaflets that are less wrinkled, and its canes tend to run along the ground rather than forming tall, arching thickets like Himalayan blackberry.



Brief History
The genus name Rubus comes from the Latin word for “bramble” or “red,” referring to the reddish color of the canes and fruits in some species.
Rubus armeniacus is native to the Caucasus region and parts of western Eurasia and was introduced to Europe and North America in the late 1800s as a cultivated fruit plant, partly popularized by the plant breeder Luther Burbank. It escaped cultivation and spread aggressively along roadsides, riverbanks, and disturbed ground throughout the Pacific Northwest. Historically, Himalayan blackberry symbolizes abundance and entanglement: abundance for its heavy fruiting, and entanglement or protection for its dense, thorny thickets.



Fun Facts

Himalayan blackberry is now so widespread in the Willamette Valley that many people assume it is a native plant, even though it arrived relatively recently.
In European folklore, walking through or under an arch of brambles was sometimes used as a folk “cleansing” rite, believed to remove illness or bad luck as the thorns symbolically scratched away misfortune.
Despite its invasive status, the berries are prized by foragers and wildlife alike; many birds, bears, and coyotes rely on them as a major late‑summer food source.




Usage / Toxicity
Rubus armeniacus is not considered toxic. The ripe berries are widely eaten fresh, made into jams, pies, wines, and syrups, and are rich in vitamins and antioxidants. Young shoots can be peeled and eaten like a vegetable when very tender. Leaves have been used in traditional herbal teas for mild astringent and anti‑diarrheal effects, though they should be used in moderation and proper identification is essential. The main “hazard” is mechanical: the strong, hooked thorns can tear clothing and skin and can impede movement for people and animals.



Invasiveness / Conservation Status
Rubus armeniacus is highly invasive in the Pacific Northwest; it forms dense, nearly impenetrable thickets that shade out native shrubs, tree seedlings, and understory plants, and it can alter streamside habitats important for salmon and other wildlife. To control it, cut canes repeatedly during the growing season and dig out or grub up the root crowns where feasible; covering cut patches with thick mulch or black plastic for one or more growing seasons can help exhaust root reserves. Follow‑up pulling of new sprouts is essential. When removing Himalayan blackberry, it’s helpful to replant with competitive native shrubs (such as salmonberry, snowberry, or osoberry) to prevent it from quickly re‑invading.




Bonus
I’m reposting this comparison picture that shows the differences in the leaves of the Himalayan blackberry, the California Blackberry and poison oak. The California blackberry has 3 small leaflets, green on both sides, and a round stem with many small straight prickles. Himalayan blackberry has 3-5 large leaflets with white undersides and a 5-angled stem with large, sharp, curved, and widely-spaced prickles. Poison oak also has leaflets of three, but the leaves are not hairy. You can use the fun mnemonic – “Leaves of three, let it be; If it’s shiny, watch your hiney; If it’s hairy, it’s a berry” [source]



Comparison

title: Brambles Part 3
text:
Since the last two posts were about blackberries, I figured we could stay on the same topic and see what other berries are growing in our greenspace this spring. In addition to the California and Himalayan blackberries, there are many other varieties of berry-bearing brambles that grow in the Pacific Northwest forests. Many of them look very similar to blackberries, with clusters of smaller jagged leaves and thorny vines and little white or pink flowers. But not all berries grow on vines, some grow on shrubs, and some, like the one we saw on our walk today, have large lobed leaves like a maple: the great Thimbleberry!



Thimbleberry



Thimbleberry
The plant in this image is a Rubus parviflorus, commonly called Thimbleberry, also known as Western thimbleberry, or Mountain sorrel. It is in the rose family (Rosaceae) and is native to western North America, including the Pacific Northwest.
You can tell this is a Thimbleberry because of these features:

Large, soft, maple‑like leaves with 5 shallow lobes and a slightly wrinkled surface

Leaves and stems lack the prickles or thorns typical of most Rubus (berry) species

Delicate, five‑petaled white flower with a golden-yellow cluster of stamens in the center

Upright, cane‑like stems growing in a shady, moist forest understory typical of the Willamette Valley



Similar plants
The Thimbleberry could sometimes be confused with Salmonberry which also has white (or pink) flowers and grows as a cane-forming shrub in moist Pacific Northwest woods. It also has edible raspberry-like fruits. However, Salmonberry is covered in noticeable prickles, usually has smaller and more triangular leaves divided into three main sections, and its flowers are often a deeper pink to magenta rather than pure white.



Brief History
The genus name Rubus comes from the Latin word for “bramble” or “blackberry,” referring to the many shrubby, often thorny berry species in this group.
Rubus parviflorus is native to western North America from Alaska down through California and east into the Rocky Mountains, where it has long been an important wild fruit for Indigenous peoples. The English common name “thimbleberry” comes from the hollow, thimble‑shaped berries that detach easily from the core, resembling a sewing thimble. Historically, the plant has been used both as a food and medicine, and its soft leaves were used practically as makeshift “toilet paper” or food wrappers. Symbolically, Thimbleberry is sometimes associated with abundance and the sweetness of summer in regional folklore because of its prolific fruiting in undisturbed forest edges.



Fun Facts

Indigenous groups such as the Coast Salish, Tlingit, and others ate the berries fresh, dried them into cakes, and sometimes mixed them with other fruits like salal to store for winter.

The broad, velvety leaves were used as natural food wrappers, improvised plates, and even as a soft substitute for toilet paper by campers and hikers.

In some local traditions, carrying or planting thimbleberry near a home was thought to invite good harvests and protection for travelers, tying the plant to ideas of safe journeys and continued bounty.




Usage / Toxicity
Thimbleberry is not considered toxic. The bright red berries are edible, though very soft and seedy, and are best eaten fresh off the plant or made into jams and jellies where their tart flavor shines. Young shoots have reportedly been eaten peeled and raw or lightly cooked in some Indigenous cuisines. Medicinally, different Indigenous peoples have used leaf or bark infusions for a variety of ailments, including gastrointestinal upsets and to aid childbirth, though such uses should be approached cautiously and with proper ethnobotanical guidance. The shrub is also valuable for habitat restoration, stabilizing slopes, and providing food and cover for birds, bears, and other wildlife.



Invasiveness / Conservation Status
Thimbleberry is not invasive; it is a native forest shrub in the Pacific Northwest and much of western North America. It can spread vigorously by rhizomes and form dense thickets, but this is generally ecologically beneficial, providing erosion control and wildlife habitat. In gardens or restoration sites, you can manage its spread by thinning canes and installing root barriers if needed. Conservation-wise, it is not considered threatened, but preserving moist forest edges, riparian corridors, and limiting large-scale clearing helps maintain healthy thimbleberry populations and the native ecosystems they support.
